elements.sent-2411-001: This paper introduces an innovative approach to radio astronomy by utilizing the global network of Internet of Things (IoT) devices to form a distributed radio telescope.
elements.sent-2411-002: Leveraging existing IoT infrastructure with minimal modifications, the proposed system employs widely dispersed devices to simultaneously capture both astronomical and communication signals.
elements.sent-2411-003: Digital beamforming techniques are applied to align the astronomical signals, effectively minimizing interference from communication sources.
elements.sent-2411-004: Calibration is achieved using multiple distributed satellites transmitting known signals, enabling precise channel estimation and phase correction via GPS localization.
elements.sent-2411-005: We analyze two calibration methods—Phase Alignment Calibration (PAC) and Eigenvalue-Based Calibration (EVC)—and demonstrate that EVC outperforms PAC in environments with significant variation in node performance.
elements.sent-2411-006: Compared to the Green Bank Telescope (GBT), the IoT-based telescope enhances antenna gain by three orders of magnitude and increases survey speed by eight orders of magnitude, owing to the vast number of nodes and expansive field of view (FoV).
elements.sent-2411-007: These findings demonstrate the feasibility and significant advantages of the IoT-enabled telescope, paving the way for cost-effective, high-speed, and widely accessible astronomical observations.
elements.sent-2411-008: Central to this field are radio telescopes, which typically employ parabolic or spherical reflector antennas to capture faint astronomical signals.
elements.sent-2411-009: These signals are focused onto a feed-receiving system at the reflector's focal point for analysis.
elements.sent-2411-010: Maintaining a high signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) amidst faint cosmic radio signals, often ranging from $-60$ dB to $-30$ dB, necessitates large reflector antennas and highly sensitive detection systems.
elements.sent-2411-011: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:current_telescope_issue , this requirement has led to the construction of major observatories, such as the 305-meter Arecibo radio telescope, built in 1963 at an inflation-adjusted cost of approximately \$64 million~\cite{cost_arecibo , and China's 500-meter Aperture Spherical Telescope (FAST), completed in 2017 for around \$180 million~\cite{cost_fast .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: These projects reflect a trend toward larger astronomical instruments; however, costs increase exponentially with the size of the reflector antenna~\cite{hansen1981fundamental, davis2011fundamental, geyi2003physical .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Furthermore, modern wireless communication networks, which utilize an increasing number of spectrum bands, pose significant challenges to the operations of passive radio telescopes.
elements.sent-2411-014: To protect facilities like the Green Bank Observatory, the United States established the National Radio Quiet Zone (NRQZ)~\cite{kellermann2020nrao, abidin2021radio, kellermann2020largest , encompassing 13,000 square miles.}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, the proliferation of wireless devices and smart city technologies near NRQZ boundaries in Fig.~\ref{fig:current_telescope_issue could lead to increased interference~\cite{beaudet2013radio, abidin2021radio, kellermann2020nrao, sizemore1991national .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Additionally, economic development near radio quiet zones is restricted to preserve astronomical observations, limiting local economic opportunities.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Figures/current_telescope_issue.png} \caption{ Challenges faced by current radio telescopes.} \label{fig:current_telescope_issue}
elements.sent-2411-017: This paper introduces an innovative concept that leverages the global deployment of Internet of Things (IoT) devices to develop a groundbreaking radio telescope, utilizing satellites for calibration.
elements.sent-2411-018: The proposed system integrates with existing IoT infrastructure and communication networks with minimal modifications.
elements.sent-2411-019: By sharing the same frequency, time, and location as communication signals, the radio telescope requires no additional hardware or frequency bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-020: The inspiration for this ambitious project arises from critical technological and economic trends expected to transform communication and astronomical observation, as depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:motivation .}
elements.sent-2411-021: IoT Expansion: The rapidly increasing number of IoT-connected devices is fundamental to this proposal.
elements.sent-2411-022: Projections suggest that the global count of IoT devices could exceed 100 billion by 2050~\cite{karunarathne2018wireless .}
elements.sent-2411-023: This vast network of devices, each capable of sending and receiving signals, presents a unique opportunity to create a distributed sensor network acting collectively as a large-scale radio telescope.
elements.sent-2411-024: Advancements in Connectivity: Propelled by developments in 6G/7G networks and advanced fiber optics, average global internet speeds are expected to surpass 30 Gbps by 2050~\cite{internetspeed .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Such enhancements will support the swift transfer of substantial volumes of observational data across this global network to centralized data centers and supercomputing facilities.
elements.sent-2411-026: Reduced Satellite Costs: The significant reduction in satellite launch costs into low Earth orbit—from the era of the Space Shuttle to future projections involving space elevators by 2050~\cite{satellite_cost —has made space-based platforms increasingly accessible.}
elements.sent-2411-027: This trend is poised to facilitate the deployment of specialized satellites that not only calibrate but also substantially expand the field of view of the IoT-enabled radio telescope.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{Figures/motivation.png} \caption{ The proposed IoT-enabled radio telescope concept.} \label{fig:motivation}
elements.sent-2411-028: Traditional radio telescopes encounter substantial challenges in spectrum sharing primarily due to their structural design and operational environments~\cite{jaroenjittichai2017radio, baan2004radio, bolli2013mobile, briggs2000removing, kesteven2007radio .}
elements.sent-2411-029: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:spectrum_sharing (left), these instruments simultaneously capture both astronomical and wireless communication signals at the same frequencies.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Due to the physical limitations of using a solid, large single reflector antenna system, they lack the ability to process or differentiate these signals independently~\cite{leshem2000multichannel, briggs2005overview, fridman2001rfi, bhat2005radio, an2017radio, joardar2007design .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Consequently, communication signals often disrupt astronomical observations, introducing noise that can obscure or distort valuable scientific data~\cite{lanzerotti2001space, penzias1973millimeter, leshem2000radio, national2010spectrum, bean2022casa, emrich1993suggestions, dai2019spectrum .}
elements.sent-2411-032: The proposed radio telescope design in Fig.~\ref{fig:spectrum_sharing (right) addresses these limitations by employing a network of widely dispersed IoT devices.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Each device is configured to concurrently capture both astronomical and communication signals at the same frequency, time, and location.
elements.sent-2411-034: These signals are then processed using digital beamforming techniques to phase-align the astronomical signals and maximize the system's output SNR.
elements.sent-2411-035: Additionally, since the SNR for most astronomical signals is below 0 dB, a threshold near the receiver's noise floor can be implemented to suppress interference from stronger communication signals.
elements.sent-2411-036: This strategic processing enables the network to focus on and amplify the astronomical signals while effectively minimizing interference from communication signals, which are treated as background noise and subsequently minimized from the final data output.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.99\textwidth]{Figures/spectrum_sharing.png} \caption{ Enabled spectrum sharing: (left) traditional radio telescope limitations; (right) proposed IoT-enabled telescope facilitating spectrum sharing.} \label{fig:spectrum_sharing}
elements.sent-2411-037: This capacity for efficient spectrum sharing between astronomical and communication signals opens up groundbreaking opportunities for radio astronomy.
elements.sent-2411-038: It allows for the operation of radio telescopes in more populated or electronically active areas, overcoming a major limitation of traditional telescopes that require secluded, radio-quiet locations.
elements.sent-2411-039: Additionally, it democratizes access to radio astronomy by utilizing the widespread and readily available IoT infrastructure, thereby minimizing the necessity for costly, dedicated radio telescope facilities.
elements.sent-2411-040: The remainder of this paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-041: Section II details the system architecture and operational modes of the proposed IoT-enabled radio telescope.
elements.sent-2411-042: Section III explores the calibration methods using single and multiple satellites.
elements.sent-2411-043: In Section IV, we compare Phase Alignment Calibration (PCA) and Eigenvalue-Based Calibration (EBC) approaches.
elements.sent-2411-044: Section V presents a performance estimation and a comparative analysis with the Green Bank Telescope (GBT).
elements.sent-2411-045: Finally, Section VI concludes the paper.
elements.sent-2411-046: In this section, we present an overview of the proposed radio telescope system that leverages the global network of IoT devices.
elements.sent-2411-047: The system is designed for seamless integration with existing IoT infrastructure and communication networks, requiring only minimal modifications.
elements.sent-2411-048: Fig.~\ref{fig:system_overview illustrates the architecture of the proposed system.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Each IoT device is equipped with an antenna, low-noise amplifier (LNA), mixer, analog-to-digital converter (ADC), processing unit, and memory.
elements.sent-2411-050: These devices are capable of capturing, digitizing, processing, and temporarily storing timestamped data $\mathbf{x [i]$.}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.98\textwidth]{Figures/system_overview.png} \caption{Overview of the proposed system: (a) system architecture; (b) calibration and observation modes.} \label{fig:system_overview}
elements.sent-2411-051: At each time sample $i$, the data vector $\mathbf{x [i]$ collected by the IoT devices is represented as: \begin{equation}x[i] = a\, s[i] + \sum_{q = 1}^{Q} v_{q}[i]\, d_{q}[i] + n[i],\end{equation} where:.}
elements.sent-2411-052: The data $\mathbf{x [i]$ can be efficiently transmitted over the communication network to a centralized data center and supercomputing facility.}
elements.sent-2411-053: For non-real-time observations, transmission times can be strategically scheduled during periods when devices are typically idle or charging, such as nighttime hours.
elements.sent-2411-054: The supercomputing facility computes the time-average correlation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ using $N$ samples: \begin{equation}R = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i = 0}^{N-1} x[i]\, x[i]^{H},\end{equation} where $^{H}$ denotes the Hermitian transpose.
elements.sent-2411-055: Using this correlation matrix, the beamforming weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ is calculated in the direction of the astronomical signal $s[i]$.
elements.sent-2411-056: The combined output signal is then obtained by: \begin{equation}y[i] = w^{H} x[i].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-057: As depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:system_overview (b), the proposed system operates in two primary modes: calibration and observation.}
elements.sent-2411-058: In the calibration mode, specialized satellites act as calibrators by transmitting known calibration signals.
elements.sent-2411-059: These signals are received by the globally distributed IoT devices, enabling precise estimation of channel properties such as time delays and frequency shifts caused by the varied locations and operational environments of the devices.
elements.sent-2411-060: This process ensures synchronization and coherence of the data collected from different devices.
elements.sent-2411-061: The known calibration signals also aid in characterizing and quantifying noise levels and interference patterns in the received data, enhancing the clarity and accuracy of the subsequent astronomical observations.
elements.sent-2411-062: Calibration facilitates the fine-tuning of each IoT device's parameters, such as gain settings and filter thresholds, thereby maximizing the sensitivity of the receiver system.
elements.sent-2411-063: The calibration data and timestamps are used to compute the maximum SNR beamforming weight vector $\mathbf{w _{\text{max,SNR }$ by solving the generalized eigenvalue problem: \begin{equation}R_{sat}\, w = \lambda_{max}\, R_{n}\, w,\end{equation} where:.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:calibration_description , $\mathbf{R _{n}$ is obtained during periods when the calibration source is powered off, and $\mathbf{R _{\text{sat }$ is calculated by subtracting $\mathbf{R _{n}$ from the total received correlation matrix when the calibration source is on: \begin{equation}R_{sat} = R_{on} - R_{off}.\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Figures/calibration_description.png} \caption{Signal processing in calibration and observation modes.} \label{fig:calibration_description}
elements.sent-2411-065: Once the optimal beamforming weights $\mathbf{w _{\text{max,SNR }$ are determined, the system transitions to observation mode to detect astronomical signals.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: The output SNR in observation mode is expressed as: \begin{equation}SNR_{out} = \frac{w_{max,SNR}^{H} R_{sig}\, w_{max,SNR}}{w_{max,SNR}^{H} R_{n}\, w_{max,SNR}},\end{equation} where $\mathbf{R _{\text{sig }$ is the correlation matrix of the astronomical signal.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Due to variations in signal and noise caused by environmental factors, the system periodically alternates between calibration and observation modes to maintain accuracy and coherence in data collection.
elements.sent-2411-068: To enhance the efficiency of correlation and beamforming computations, a multi-layer digital beamforming strategy is employed, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:system_overview (a) right.}
elements.sent-2411-069: This approach divides the global network of IoT devices into numerous smaller regions where initial beamforming weights are calculated locally.
elements.sent-2411-070: These locally beamformed signals are then aggregated and further processed through a secondary layer of digital beamforming.
elements.sent-2411-071: This multi-tiered method optimizes computational distribution and improves signal precision by reducing the computational burden on individual nodes and minimizing the required data transmission bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-072: By processing data hierarchically, the system enhances overall processing efficiency and scalability.
elements.sent-2411-073: Utilizing multiple satellites for calibration allows the system to perform beam scanning in multiple directions simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-074: Each satellite can be dedicated to calibrating a specific beam, and by increasing the number of satellites, the system can effectively generate a vast number of beams concurrently.
elements.sent-2411-075: When combined with advanced digital beamforming algorithms, this configuration enables the creation of infinite beams theoretically, significantly enhancing the telescope's flexibility and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-076: The ability to calibrate and observe multiple beams concurrently increases the system's capacity for comprehensive sky surveys, facilitating high-resolution imaging and rapid detection of astronomical events.
elements.sent-2411-077: This multi-beam functionality is crucial for monitoring transient phenomena and conducting large-scale mapping of the sky.
elements.sent-2411-078: An essential advantage of the proposed IoT-based radio telescope is its ability to operate alongside existing communication signals in the same frequency bands, times, and locations without requiring additional hardware.
elements.sent-2411-079: Since the SNR for most astronomical signals is below $0\,\text{dB}$, implementing a threshold near the receiver's noise floor suppresses interference from stronger communication signals.
elements.sent-2411-080: This approach effectively filters out signals exceeding the noise threshold, allowing the network to focus on amplifying the weak astronomical signals.
elements.sent-2411-081: Due to the large spacing between distributed nodes, communication signals at the array output can be treated as background noise.
elements.sent-2411-082: The system minimizes their impact through strategic digital signal processing.
elements.sent-2411-083: By coherently combining weak astronomical signals using digital beamforming, it enhances their detectability despite the presence of stronger interference.
elements.sent-2411-084: This capability enables efficient spectrum sharing between astronomical observations and communication services.
elements.sent-2411-085: IoT devices can simultaneously perform their primary communication functions while contributing to the radio telescope's data collection, leveraging existing infrastructure and frequency allocations without the need for dedicated hardware or exclusive frequency bands.
elements.subsection-2411-008: Non-Technical Consideration: Public Engagement and Incentive Plan
elements.sent-2411-086: To enhance public engagement and maximize the operational efficiency of the distributed IoT-based radio telescope network, we could have an incentive plan encouraging individuals to contribute their personal devices, such as smartphones and laptops, as active nodes in the array.
elements.sent-2411-087: This transforms everyday devices into valuable assets for astronomical research.
elements.sent-2411-088: The plan offers compensation to participants through digital credits, service discounts, or payments based on their contribution level, measured by processing power utilized and duration of device engagement.
elements.sent-2411-089: This approach ensures fair compensation while reducing operational costs associated with traditional radio telescopes.
elements.sent-2411-090: By leveraging the ubiquity of personal devices and existing connectivity, it minimizes the need for extensive physical infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-091: Additionally, data transfers can be scheduled during off-peak internet usage hours and lower electricity cost periods, further reducing expenses.
elements.sent-2411-092: Participants can choose their level of involvement to match personal preferences, enabling a broader community to participate as citizen scientists.
elements.sent-2411-093: Incorporating this incentive plan not only enhances the network's scalability but also fosters public collaboration in scientific discovery.
elements.sent-2411-094: This democratization of astronomical research can increase public interest in science and technology, providing educational opportunities and promoting STEM engagement across diverse populations.
elements.section-2411-003: Node Phase Calibration Using Satellites for Simultaneous Multiple Beams
elements.sent-2411-095: In this section, we introduce a calibration method for large-scale distributed node arrays on Earth's surface utilizing multiple calibration satellites, designated as Satellite B.
elements.sent-2411-096: This technique significantly improves array gain and reduces phase errors compared to methods that rely on a single calibration satellite (Satellite A).
elements.sent-2411-097: By deploying multiple satellites for calibration, the system enables simultaneous beam scanning in various directions, theoretically allowing the generation of an infinite number of beams when integrated with digital beamforming algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-098: We consider a large aperture array consisting of $N_{\text{node }$ nodes randomly distributed along a circular path on Earth's surface.}
elements.sent-2411-099: For computational simplicity, we model the array in a two-dimensional plane while accounting for Earth's curvature.
elements.sent-2411-101: Each node is assumed to be an isotropic radiator, a common assumption in array antenna theory to simplify analysis \cite{balanis2016antenna .}
elements.sent-2411-102: Due to the large separation distances between nodes, mutual coupling effects are neglected.
elements.sent-2411-103: The array operates at a central frequency of $f = 1.42$\,GHz, corresponding to the resonant frequency of neutral hydrogen, which is significant in radio astronomy.
elements.sent-2411-104: The nodes are intended to form a large aperture array with a baseline length $L_{\text{baseline }$.}
elements.sent-2411-105: The steering angle $\theta_{\text{steer }$ ranges from $-90^\circ$ to $90^\circ$, allowing for beam scanning across a wide angular range.}
elements.sent-2411-106: Two sets of calibration satellites are employed:.
elements.sent-2411-107: Satellite A: A single calibration satellite positioned at an altitude of $h_{\text{satA }$ above Earth's surface.}
elements.sent-2411-108: Satellite B: Multiple distributed calibration satellites, with a total number of $N_{\text{satB }$, evenly distributed along an orbital path at an altitude of $h_{\text{satB }$.}}
elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{center} \includegraphics[scale=0.4]{Figures/schematic_baseline} \caption{Schematic of active array nodes for beamforming in a 2-D plane.} \label{fig:schematic_baseline} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-109: When the beam is steered in the $\theta_{\text{steer }$ direction, the active nodes for beamforming described in Fig.~\ref{fig:schematic_baseline are selected within a angle range defined by $\theta_{\text{steer \pm \Delta\theta$, where \begin{equation}\Delta\theta = \arcsin\left(\frac{L_{\text{baseline}}}{2 R_{\text{earth}}}\right).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: This ensures that the nodes included in the calibration are within the desired baseline length.
elements.sent-2411-111: The number of active nodes is calculated as \begin{equation}N_{\text{active}} = \left\lfloor \frac{2 \Delta\theta}{2\pi} N_{\text{node}} \right\rfloor.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-112: Assuming isotropic sources and coherent summation, the ideal array gain is given by \begin{equation}G_{\text{ideal}} = 10 \log_{10} N_{\text{active}}^{2}. \label{eq:ideal_gain}\end{equation}.
elements.subsection-2411-011: Calibration Using Single Calibration Satellite (Satellite A)
elements.sent-2411-113: In the this calibration method, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:schematic_satA , a single calibration satellite (Satellite A) is used to send calibration signals to each node.}
elements.sent-2411-114: The calibration process involves determining the relative phase delays between nodes based on the received calibration signals.
elements.figure-2411-007: \begin{center} \includegraphics[scale=0.4]{Figures/schematic_satA} \caption{Schematic for node calibration using single calibration satellite (Satellite A).} \label{fig:schematic_satA} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-115: The distance between Satellite A and the $n$-th node is denoted as $d_{\text{satA-node ,n}$, and the corresponding calibration phase shift is calculated by \begin{equation}\phi_{\text{cal,satA},n} = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} d_{\text{satA-node},n}, \quad n = 1, 2, \dots, N_{\text{active}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-116: We define the angular difference between each node and the steering direction as \begin{equation}\Delta\theta_n = \theta_{\text{steer}} - \theta_n,\end{equation} where $\theta_n$ is the angular position of the $n$-th node.
elements.sent-2411-117: The geometric phase term due to Earth's curvature is given by \begin{equation}\phi_{\text{geom,satA},n} = -\frac{2\pi}{\lambda} R_{\text{earth}} \left(1 - \cos\Delta\theta_n\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-118: Assuming isotropic sources and applying uniform weighting, the array factor after calibration with Satellite A is \begin{equation}AF_{\text{satA}} = \sum_{n=1}^{N_{\text{active}}} \exp\left( j \phi_{\text{geom,satA},n} + j \phi_{\text{cal,satA},n} \right). \label{eq:AFsatA}\end{equation} The array gain is calculated as the normalized magnitude squared of the array factor: \begin{equation}G_{\text{satA}} = 10 \log_{10} \left| AF_{\text{satA}} \right|^2 .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-119: In the far-field region (when the distance between Satellite A and Earth is large), the calibration phase $\phi_{\text{cal,satA ,n}$ can be used to well compensate the geometric phase $\phi_{\text{geom,satA ,n}$ variation among the nodes.}}
elements.sent-2411-120: In this case, the array gain $G_{\text{satA $ is approximately equal to the ideal array gain $G_{\text{ideal }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: This calibration approach relies on a single satellite to receive astronomical signals from a specific direction.
elements.sent-2411-122: To achieve simultaneous multiple scanned beams, multiple satellites like Satellite A would be required.
elements.sent-2411-123: In sub-regions where the coverage of different satellites overlaps, like the overlap of red and blue sub-regions in Fig.~\ref{fig:schematic_satA , the calibration signals can be separated by employing different frequencies or coding schemes to avoid ambiguity.}
elements.subsection-2411-012: Calibration Using Multiple Distributed Calibration Satellites (Satellite B)
elements.sent-2411-124: To enhance the calibration process, we introduce multiple distributed calibration satellites (Satellite B) in Fig.~\ref{fig:schematic_satB .}
elements.sent-2411-125: Each satellite in Satellite B is responsible for calibrating a specific sub-region of nodes.
elements.sent-2411-126: The direction from the center of each node sub-region to its corresponding satellite is aligned with the beam steering direction.
elements.figure-2411-008: \begin{center} \includegraphics[scale=0.39]{Figures/schematic_satB} \caption{Schematic for node calibration using multiple distributed calibration satellites (Satellite B).} \label{fig:schematic_satB} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-127: The angular positions of the satellites in Satellite B are defined as \begin{equation}\theta_{\text{satB},k} = -\pi + \left( k - \frac{1}{2} \right) \frac{2\pi}{N_{\text{satB}}}, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, N_{\text{satB}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-128: For the $n$-th node in the $k$-th sub-region, the calibration phase involves calculating the relative phase variation due different distance $d_{\text{satB-node ,n}$ between the node and its corresponding satellite and relative phase $\phi_{\text{satB-steer ,k}$ among each satellites.}}
elements.sent-2411-129: The calibration phase shift is \begin{equation}\phi_{\text{cal,satB},n} = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} d_{\text{satB-node},n} + \phi_{\text{satB-steer},k}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-130: Here, $\phi_{\text{satB-steer ,k}$ accounts for the phase difference between the $k$-th satellite and the plane wavefront in the steering direction, calculated by \begin{equation}\phi_{\text{satB-steer},k} = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \left( h_{\text{satB}} + R_{\text{earth}} \right) \left( 1 - \cos \theta_{\text{satB-steer},k} \right),\end{equation} where $\theta_{\text{satB-steer ,k}$ is the angle difference between the $k$-th satellite and the steering direction: \begin{equation}\theta_{\text{satB-steer},k} = \theta_{\text{steer}} - \theta_{\text{satB},k}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-131: The geometric phase term for nodes due to Earth's curvature is \begin{equation}\phi_{\text{geom},n} = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} R_{\text{earth}} \cos \Delta\theta_n.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-132: Assuming isotropic sources and uniform weighting, the array factor after calibration with Satellite B is \begin{equation}AF_{\text{satB}} = \sum_{k=1}^{N_{\text{satB}}} \sum_{n \in \mathcal{R}_k} \exp\left( j \phi_{\text{geom},n} + j \phi_{\text{cal,satB},n} \right), \label{eq:AFsatB}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{R _k$ represents the set of nodes in the $k$-th sub-region associated with the $k$-th satellite.}
elements.sent-2411-133: The array gain is then calculated as \begin{equation}G_{\text{satB}} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \left| AF_{\text{satB}} \right|^2 \right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-134: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:schematic_satB , the satellites in Satellite B and the nodes can be multiplexed for different scanning beams.}
elements.sent-2411-135: As the beam is steered, the node regions and corresponding satellites can be redefined, allowing for flexible beamforming capabilities.
elements.subsection-2411-013: Phase Error Mitigation Using GPS Localization
elements.sent-2411-136: Unlike astronomical signals originating from sources at infinite distances, the finite distance of Satellite B introduces phase variations in the wavefront curvature across the array, leading to phase discrepancies among the nodes.
elements.sent-2411-137: To mitigate these phase errors, we employ GPS localization to estimate the positions of both the nodes and the satellites, allowing us to apply appropriate phase corrections during calibration.
elements.sent-2411-138: Each node and satellite may have position estimation errors due to limitations in GPS accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-139: Let $\Delta_{\text{node,GPS }$ and $\Delta_{\text{sat }$ represent the maximum position errors for the nodes and satellites, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-140: The estimated positions are obtained by adding random errors within these bounds to the true positions.
elements.sent-2411-141: For the $n$-th node with true position ($x_{\text{node ,n}$, $z_{\text{node ,n}$) in the $k$-th node sub-region, the estimated position is \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} x_{\text{node},n}^{\text{est}} = x_{\text{node},n} + \delta x_{\text{node},n}, z_{\text{node},n}^{\text{est}} = z_{\text{node},n} + \delta z_{\text{node},n}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\delta x_{\text{node ,n}$ and $\delta z_{\text{node ,n}$ are random variables uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta_{\text{node,GPS , \Delta_{\text{node,GPS ]$.}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-022: \begin{equation} x_{node,n}^{est} = x_{node,n} + \delta x_{node,n}, z_{node,n}^{est} = z_{node,n} + \delta z_{node,n}, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-142: Similarly, the estimated position of the $k$-th satellite with true position ($x_{\text{satB ,k}$, $z_{\text{satB ,k}$) is \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} x_{\text{satB},k}^{\text{est}} = x_{\text{satB},k} + \delta x_{\text{sat},k}, z_{\text{satB},k}^{\text{est}} = z_{\text{satB},k} + \delta z_{\text{sat},k}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\delta x_{\text{sat ,k}$ and $\delta z_{\text{sat ,k}$ are random variables uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta_{\text{sat , \Delta_{\text{sat ]$.}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-023: \begin{equation} x_{satB,k}^{est} = x_{satB,k} + \delta x_{sat,k}, z_{satB,k}^{est} = z_{satB,k} + \delta z_{sat,k}, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-143: In practice, the satellite position can be estimated with an accuracy better than 1 centimeter~\cite{rudenko2023radial .}
elements.sent-2411-144: Therefore, we can assume that the true satellite position is effectively known and set $\Delta_{\text{sat }$ to zero.}
elements.sent-2411-145: Using the estimated positions, we calculate the estimated distance between each node and its corresponding satellite \begin{equation}d_{\text{satB-node},n}^{\text{est}} = \sqrt{\left( x_{\text{satB},k}^{\text{est}} - x_{\text{node},n}^{\text{est}} \right)^2 + \left( z_{\text{satB},k}^{\text{est}} - z_{\text{node},n}^{\text{est}} \right)^2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-146: We also calculate the estimated radial distance of the node from Earth's center \begin{equation}r_{\text{node},n}^{\text{est}} = \sqrt{\left( x_{\text{node},n}^{\text{est}} \right)^2 + \left( z_{\text{node},n}^{\text{est}} \right)^2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-147: The estimated angular positions of the node and satellite are \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \theta_{\text{node},n}^{\text{est}} = \arctan\left( x_{\text{node},n}^{\text{est}}, z_{\text{node},n}^{\text{est}} \right), \theta_{\text{satB},k}^{\text{est}} = \arctan\left( x_{\text{satB},k}^{\text{est}}, z_{\text{satB},k}^{\text{est}} \right). \end{aligned}\end{equation} The estimated angle between the node and its corresponding satellite is \begin{equation}\Delta\theta_{\text{satB-node},n}^{\text{est}} = \theta_{\text{satB},k}^{\text{est}} - \theta_{\text{node},n}^{\text{est}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-148: We compute an intermediate angle $\alpha_n$ using the Law of Sines \begin{equation}\alpha_n = \arcsin\left( \frac{r_{\text{node},n}^{\text{est}}}{d_{\text{satB-node},n}^{\text{est}}} \sin\Delta\theta_{\text{satB-node},n}^{\text{est}} \right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-149: The estimated angle between the satellite and the steering direction is \begin{equation}\Delta\theta_{\text{satB-steer},k}^{\text{est}} = \theta_{\text{steer}} - \theta_{\text{satB},k}^{\text{est}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-150: The angle between the node-satellite line and the steering direction is then \begin{equation}\beta_n = \alpha_n - \Delta\theta_{\text{satB-steer},k}^{\text{est}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-151: The excess path length due to the angle $\beta_n$ is calculated by \begin{equation}\delta d_{\text{GPS},n} = d_{\text{satB-node},n}^{\text{est}} \left( 1 - \cos \beta_n \right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-152: The GPS correction phase is then \begin{equation}\phi_{\text{GPS},n} = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \delta d_{\text{GPS},n}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-153: The corrected calibration phase for each node is \begin{equation}\phi_{\text{cal,GPS},n} = \phi_{\text{cal,satB},n} - \phi_{\text{GPS},n},\end{equation} where $\phi_{\text{cal,satB ,n}$ is the original calibration phase without GPS correction, given by \begin{equation}\phi_{\text{cal,satB},n} = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} d_{\text{satB-node},n} + \phi_{\text{satB-steer},k}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-154: The array factor after incorporating GPS-based correction is \begin{equation}AF_{\text{satB,GPS}} = \sum_{k=1}^{N_{\text{satB}}} \sum_{n \in \mathcal{R}_k} \exp\left( j \phi_{\text{geom},n} + j \phi_{\text{cal,GPS},n} \right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-155: The array gain with GPS correction is then \begin{equation}G_{\text{satB,GPS}} = 10 \log_{10} \left| AF_{\text{satB,GPS}} \right|^2 .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-156: In summary, the finite distance of Satellite B causes the incoming wavefront to have curvature, leading to phase discrepancies among the nodes when compared to plane wavefronts from astronomical sources at infinite distances.
elements.sent-2411-157: By using GPS estimates of the node and satellite positions, we calculate the angle between the node-satellite line and the steering direction, $\beta_n$.
elements.sent-2411-158: This angle results in an excess path length $\delta d_{\text{GPS ,n}$, which introduces phase errors in the calibration process.}
elements.sent-2411-159: By computing $\delta d_{\text{GPS ,n}$ and the corresponding correction phase $\phi_{\text{GPS ,n}$, we adjust the calibration phase for each node to account for these errors, effectively aligning the phase of the received signals as if they were from an astronomical source at infinite distance.}}

elements.sent-2411-208: In summary, the analysis results validate the effectiveness of the proposed phase calibration method using multiple distributed calibration satellites (Satellite B) with GPS localization.. Key findings include:.
elements.sent-2411-209: Proposed Multi-Satellite Calibration with GPS Correction: Employing multiple distributed calibration satellites (Satellite B) combined with GPS localization effectively addresses the limitations of single-satellite calibration by mitigating phase errors and enhancing array performance.
elements.sent-2411-210: Effectiveness of GPS Correction: GPS localization significantly enhances the array gain by correcting phase errors arising from position uncertainties and the finite distances of calibration satellites.
elements.sent-2411-211: This correction brings the array's performance close to the ideal case, ensuring accurate phase alignment across the array.
elements.sent-2411-212: Impact of Number of Satellites: Increasing the number of calibration satellites improves the array gain, especially over longer baseline lengths.
elements.sent-2411-213: A larger number of satellites reduces phase errors associated with wavefront curvature and node position inaccuracies by dividing the array into smaller, more manageable sub-regions.
elements.sent-2411-214: Benefits of Higher Satellite Altitudes: Deploying satellites at higher altitudes reduces wavefront curvature effects, leading to improved array gain.
elements.sent-2411-215: This benefit is amplified when combined with a larger number of satellites, as the flatter wavefronts enhance phase alignment across the array.
elements.sent-2411-216: Baseline Length Considerations: Although longer baseline lengths introduce more significant phase errors, these can be effectively mitigated by increasing both the number of satellites and their altitudes, along with utilizing GPS correction.
elements.sent-2411-217: Enhanced Beamforming Capabilities: This method facilitates a nearly isotropic gain response continuously over the full scanning range.
elements.sent-2411-218: It enables the simultaneous implementation of infinite beams in theory using digital beamforming techniques, surpassing the limitations of traditional radio telescopes that are typically restricted to a limited number of simultaneous beams.
elements.sent-2411-219: These results underscore the potential of the proposed calibration method for practical applications requiring precise beamforming and high-resolution imaging in large-scale distributed arrays.
elements.section-2411-004: Calibration Method Performance Under Node Variability
elements.sent-2411-220: In this section, we analyze and compare the performance of two calibration methods for large-scale distributed arrays in the presence of variability in node gains due to different path losses and system noise figures at each node.
elements.sent-2411-221: The methods under consideration are the Phase Alignment Calibration and the Eigenvalue-Based Calibration.
elements.sent-2411-222: We present a detailed mathematical model and discuss the implications of the results.
elements.sent-2411-223: We consider a linear array of $N$ nodes distributed along the $x$-axis over a total aperture length $L$.
elements.sent-2411-224: The nodes receive both a strong calibration signal and a weak astronomical signal.
elements.sent-2411-225: Each node is equipped with a receiver characterized by an antenna gain $G_n$ and a noise figure $NF_n$, which exhibit variability to simulate real-world hardware inconsistencies.
elements.sent-2411-226: The key signal parameters include the center frequency $f_c$, the wavelength $\lambda_c = c / f_c$ (where $c$ is the speed of light), the bandwidth $B$, the calibration signal power density $P_{\text{cal }$, and the astronomical signal power density $P_{\text{astro }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-227: The positions of the nodes are given by \begin{equation}x_n = L \times (u_n - 0.5),\end{equation} where $u_n$ is a uniform random variable over $[0,1]$, representing the random distribution of nodes along the array.
elements.sent-2411-228: The antenna gains $G_n$ can vary due to different blockage effects at each node and are expressed in decibels (dB) as \begin{equation}G_n^{\text{dB}} = \overline{G}_{\text{ant}} + \sigma_{G} \, z_n^{(G)},\end{equation} where $\overline{G _{\text{ant }$ is the mean antenna gain in dB, $\sigma_{G}$ is the standard deviation, and $z_n^{(G)}$ is a standard normal random variable.}}
elements.sent-2411-229: Similarly, the noise figures $NF_n$ (in dB) are modeled as \begin{equation}NF_n^{\text{dB}} = \overline{NF} + \sigma_{NF} \, z_n^{(NF)},\end{equation} where $\overline{NF}$ is the mean noise figure in dB, $\sigma_{NF}$ is the standard deviation, and $z_n^{(NF)}$ is a standard normal random variable.
elements.sent-2411-230: The effective antenna area for the $n$-th node is calculated using \begin{equation}A_{\text{eff},n} = \frac{G_n \lambda_c^2}{4\pi},\end{equation} where $G_n$ is the linear antenna gain.
elements.sent-2411-231: The noise power at the $n$-th node is given by \begin{equation}P_{noise,n} = k_B T_{\text{noise},n} B,\end{equation} where $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant, $B$ is the bandwidth, and the noise temperature $T_{\text{noise ,n}$ is \begin{equation}T_{\text{noise},n} = T_0 \times NF_n,\end{equation} with $T_0$ being the reference temperature (290\,K) and $NF_n$ is the noise figure in linear scale.}
elements.sent-2411-232: The direction of arrival (DoA) for the astronomical signal is denoted by $\theta_s$, and the wave number is \begin{equation}k_c = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda_c}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-233: The total calibration time is denoted by $T_{\text{cal }$, which is divided into a calibration-on duration $T_{\text{cal-on }$ (when the calibration signal is present) and a calibration-off duration $T_{\text{cal-off = T_{\text{cal - T_{\text{cal-on }$ (when the calibration signal is absent).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-234: The observation time is denoted by $T_{\text{obs }$.}
elements.sent-2411-235: The received signals at each node consist of the desired signal and additive noise.
elements.sent-2411-236: The variability in antenna gains and noise figures among the nodes introduces differences in the received signal amplitudes and noise levels, which the calibration methods aim to compensate for.
elements.sent-2411-237: During the calibration-on period, the received calibration signal at node $n$ is \begin{equation}s_n^{\text{cal-on}}(t) = A_n^{\text{cal}} \cos\left(2\pi f_c t - k_c x_n \cos\theta_{\text{cal}}\right),\end{equation} where $A_n^{\text{cal = \sqrt{2 P_{\text{cal A_{\text{eff ,n }$ is the amplitude, $A_{\text{eff ,n}$ is the effective antenna area, and $\theta_{\text{cal }$ is the known calibration signal's DoA.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-238: During the observation period, the received astronomical signal at node $n$ is \begin{equation}s_n^{\text{obs}}(t) = A_n^{\text{obs}} \cos\left(2\pi f_c t - k_c x_n \cos\theta_s\right),\end{equation} where $A_n^{\text{obs = \sqrt{2 P_{\text{astro A_{\text{eff ,n }$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-239: The noise at node $n$ is modeled as zero-mean Gaussian noise with variance $\sigma_n^2 = P_{noise,n}$: \begin{equation}n_n(t) \sim \mathcal{N}\left(0, \sigma_n^2\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-240: The received signals at each node are the sum of the desired signal and the noise: \begin{equation}r_n(t) = s_n(t) + n_n(t),\end{equation} where $s_n(t)$ represents either the calibration signal or the astronomical signal, depending on the time period.
elements.sent-2411-241: We compare two calibration methods: Phase Alignment Calibration and Eigenvalue-Based Calibration.
elements.sent-2411-242: In the Phase Alignment Calibration method, the relative phase differences between nodes are estimated and compensated using cross-correlation during the calibration-on period.
elements.sent-2411-243: After downconversion and low-pass filtering, the baseband signal at node $n$ during calibration-on is \begin{equation}y_n^{\text{cal-on}}(t) = A_n^{\text{cal}} e^{-j k_c x_n \cos\theta_{\text{cal}}} + \tilde{n}_n^{\text{cal-on}}(t),\end{equation} where $\tilde{n _n^{\text{cal-on (t)$ is the baseband noise.}}}
elements.sent-2411-244: The phase difference between node $n$ and a reference node (chosen as node 1) is estimated as \begin{equation}\hat{\Delta\phi}_n = \arg\left\{ \frac{1}{T_{\text{cal-on}}} \int_{0}^{T_{\text{cal-on}}} y_n^{\text{cal-on}}(t) \left( y_1^{\text{cal-on}}(t) \right)^* dt \right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-245: The beamforming weights are then calculated as \begin{equation}w_n = e^{-j \hat{\Delta\phi}_n}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-246: During observation, the weighted signals are combined to form the array output: \begin{equation}y_{\text{combined}}^{\text{PAC}}(t) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n y_n^{\text{obs}}(t),\end{equation} where $y_n^{\text{obs (t)$ is the baseband astronomical signal at node $n$ after downconversion and filtering.}}
elements.sent-2411-247: In this method, optimal beamforming weights are computed by solving a generalized eigenvalue problem using covariance matrices estimated during calibration.
elements.sent-2411-248: The noise covariance matrix $\mathbf{R _n$ is estimated during the calibration-off period: \begin{equation}\mathbf{R}_n = \frac{1}{T_{\text{cal-off}}} \int_{0}^{T_{\text{cal-off}}} \mathbf{y}^{\text{cal-off}}(t) \mathbf{y}^{\text{cal-off}}(t)^\dagger dt,\end{equation} where $\mathbf{y ^{\text{cal-off (t) = [y_1^{\text{cal-off (t), \ldots, y_N^{\text{cal-off (t)]^\top$ and $^\dagger$ denotes the Hermitian transpose.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-249: Similarly, the signal-plus-noise covariance matrix $\mathbf{R _{\text{sn }$ is estimated during the calibration-on period: \begin{equation}\mathbf{R}_{\text{sn}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{cal-on}}} \int_{0}^{T_{\text{cal-on}}} \mathbf{y}^{\text{cal-on}}(t) \mathbf{y}^{\text{cal-on}}(t)^\dagger dt.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-250: The signal covariance matrix is obtained by subtracting the noise covariance from the signal-plus-noise covariance: \begin{equation}\mathbf{R}_s = \mathbf{R}_{\text{sn}} - \mathbf{R}_n.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-251: The optimal weights $\mathbf{w}$ are determined by solving the generalized eigenvalue problem: \begin{equation}\mathbf{R}_s \mathbf{w} = \lambda_{\max} \left( \mathbf{R}_n + \epsilon \mathbf{I} \right) \mathbf{w},\end{equation} where $\epsilon$ is a regularization parameter to prevent numerical issues (set as $\epsilon = \varepsilon \cdot \text{trace (\mathbf{R _n) / N$ with $\varepsilon$ being a small constant), $\mathbf{I}$ is the identity matrix, and $\lambda_{\max}$ is the largest eigenvalue.}}
elements.sent-2411-252: The eigenvector corresponding to the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_{\max}$ is selected as the optimal weight vector.
elements.sent-2411-253: The weights are normalized to maintain consistent array gain: \begin{equation}\mathbf{w}_{\text{norm}} = \frac{\mathbf{w}}{\left( \sum_{n=1}^{N} |w_n| \right) / N}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-254: During observation, the signals are combined using the conjugate of the normalized weights: \begin{equation}y_{\text{combined}}^{\text{EVC}}(t) = \mathbf{y}^{\text{obs}}(t)^\dagger \mathbf{w}_{\text{norm}}^*,\end{equation} where $\mathbf{y ^{\text{obs (t) = [y_1^{\text{obs (t), \ldots, y_N^{\text{obs (t)]^\top$ and $^*$ denotes complex conjugation.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-255: For both methods, the array output SNR is calculated as \begin{equation}\text{SNR} = \frac{P_s}{P_n},\end{equation} where $P_s$ is the total signal power, and $P_n$ is the total noise power at the array output.
elements.sent-2411-256: The total signal power is computed as \begin{equation}P_s = \left| \int_{0}^{T_{\text{obs}}} y_{\text{combined}}(t) dt \right|^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-257: The total noise power is estimated using the variance of the combined noise-only signals: \begin{equation}P_n = \sigma_{\text{combined}}^2 \cdot T_{\text{obs}},\end{equation} where $\sigma_{\text{combined ^2$ is the variance of $y_{\text{combined (t)$ when only noise is present.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-258: We simulate the performance of both calibration methods under two different scenarios: 1.
elements.sent-2411-259: Uniform Node Characteristics: In this scenario, all nodes have identical antenna gains and noise figures, meaning there is no variability among the nodes ($\sigma_{G = 0\,\text{dB}$ and $\sigma_{NF = 0\,\text{dB}$).}}
elements.sent-2411-260: This represents an ideal case with no hardware inconsistencies.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-261: Variable Node Characteristics: In this scenario, nodes exhibit significant variability in antenna gains and noise figures, as previously described, with $\sigma_{G = 20\,\text{dB}$ and $\sigma_{NF = 4\,\text{dB}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-262: This simulates real-world conditions where variations in hardware and antenna blockage effects for receiver nodes are present.

elements.sent-2411-273: When antenna gains and noise figures are uniform across all nodes, both PAC and EVC methods effectively combine the signals coherently.
elements.sent-2411-274: In this ideal scenario, the simpler PAC method achieves near-optimal performance while requiring less computational resources and simpler hardware implementation.
elements.sent-2411-275: This makes the PAC method attractive for systems with limited resources and consistent node characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-276: However, in practical applications such as IoT-enabled telescopes, nodes often exhibit significant variability in antenna gains and noise figures due to environmental factors like building obstructions, terrain variations, and differing hardware components.
elements.sent-2411-277: These variations lead to discrepancies in signal amplitudes and noise levels across the array.
elements.sent-2411-278: The PAC method, which assumes equal contribution from all nodes after phase alignment, neglects these differences, resulting in degraded performance when nodes have varying SNRs.
elements.sent-2411-279: The EVC method, on the other hand, optimally adjusts both the phase and amplitude of the beamforming weights based on individual node characteristics by solving a generalized eigenvalue problem.
elements.sent-2411-280: This approach emphasizes nodes with higher SNRs and attenuates contributions from noisier nodes, effectively mitigating the adverse effects of hardware variability.
elements.sent-2411-281: Consequently, the EVC method achieves higher array output SNR and demonstrates better scalability and robustness for large arrays with hardware imperfections.
elements.sent-2411-282: Considering the advantages and limitations of both methods, a hybrid approach can be beneficial in practical deployments.
elements.sent-2411-283: In environments where nodes have similar output SNRs, such as outdoor and open areas with minimal obstructions, the PAC method can be employed to capitalize on its simplicity and lower resource requirements.
elements.sent-2411-284: In urban areas or regions with significant node gain and noise figure variations due to obstructions or hardware differences, the EVC method becomes essential to ensure optimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-285: By classifying nodes based on their SNR characteristics and applying the appropriate calibration method, the system can efficiently utilize resources while maintaining high performance.
elements.sent-2411-286: This adaptive approach leverages the strengths of both methods, providing a practical solution for large-scale distributed arrays in diverse environments.

elements.sent-2411-323: This paper presents a novel approach to radio astronomy by leveraging the global network of IoT devices to form a large-scale, distributed radio telescope.
elements.sent-2411-324: The proposed system addresses the limitations of traditional radio telescopes in spectrum sharing and operational environments by utilizing widely dispersed IoT devices capable of concurrent astronomical and communication signal reception.
elements.sent-2411-325: Through digital beamforming techniques and the implementation of calibration methods using multiple distributed satellites with GPS localization, the system effectively mitigates phase errors and hardware inconsistencies among nodes.
elements.sent-2411-326: Analysis results demonstrate that the Eigenvalue-Based Calibration (EVC) method outperforms the Phase Alignment Calibration (PAC) method in environments with significant node variability, highlighting the importance of optimal weight adjustments based on individual node characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-327: The comparative performance analysis with the Green Bank Telescope (GBT) underscores the potential advantages of the IoT-based telescope, particularly in terms of increasing antenna gain by three orders of magnitude and enhancing survey speed by eight orders of magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-328: The findings indicate that the IoT-enabled radio telescope concept is not only feasible but also offers substantial benefits over traditional systems.
elements.sent-2411-329: By capitalizing on existing infrastructure and advancements in connectivity, the proposed system paves the way for cost-effective, high-speed, and widely accessible astronomical observations.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
elements.sent-2411-330: A novel calibration approach is proposed using multiple distributed satellites transmitting known signals, enabling precise channel estimation and phase correction via GPS localization.
elements.sent-2411-331: A system signal model is employed to study different calibration algorithms with node hardware variations, node clock offsets, and interference mitigation.
elements.sent-2411-332: Compared to the 500-meter Aperture Spherical Telescope (FAST), the IoT-enabled telescope enhances antenna gain by two orders of magnitude and increases survey speed by seven orders of magnitude, owing to the vast number of nodes and expansive field of view (FoV).
elements.sent-2411-333: These findings demonstrate the feasibility and significant advantages of IoT telescopes, paving the way for cost-effective, high-speed, spectrum-sharing, and widespread astronomical observations.
elements.sent-2411-334: At the heart of this discipline are radio telescopes, which typically employ single reflector antenna or multi-reflector arrays to collect extremely faint astronomical signals.
elements.sent-2411-335: These signals are then directed to a feed-receiving system located at the reflector's focal point, where they undergo subsequent signal processing and analysis.
elements.sent-2411-336: Challenges for Current Radio Telescopes: Maintaining a high signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) amidst faint cosmic radio signals, often ranging from $-60$ dB to $-30$ dB, necessitates large reflector antennas and highly sensitive detection systems.
elements.sent-2411-337: This requirement has led to the construction of major observatories, such as the 305-meter Arecibo radio telescope, built in 1963 at an inflation-adjusted cost of approximately \$64 million~\cite{cost_arecibo , and China's 500-meter Aperture Spherical Telescope (FAST), completed in 2017 for around \$180 million~\cite{cost_fast .}}
elements.sent-2411-338: These projects reflect a trend toward larger astronomical instruments; however, costs increase exponentially with the size of the reflector antenna~\cite{hansen1981fundamental .}
elements.sent-2411-339: To protect facilities like the Green Bank Observatory, the United States established the National Radio Quiet Zone (NRQZ)~\cite{kellermann2020nrao , encompassing 13,000 square miles.}
elements.sent-2411-340: However, the proliferation of wireless devices and smart city technologies near NRQZ boundaries could lead to increased interference~\cite{sizemore1991national .}
elements.sent-2411-341: By integrating seamlessly with current IoT infrastructure and communication networks—with only minimal modifications—the system operates using the same frequency, time, and location as existing communication signals, thereby eliminating the need for additional hardware or bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-342: The idea is inspired by significant technological and economic trends that are set to transform both communication and astronomical observation, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:motivation .}
elements.sent-2411-343: This advancement enables the deployment of specialized calibration satellites that not only enhance system calibration but also considerably expand the field of view of the IoT radio telescope.
elements.sent-2411-344: Furthermore, for a low cost consideration, the commercial low-orbit communication satellites may also be utilized as calibration sources.

elements.sent-2411-348: Spectrum Sharing: Traditional radio telescopes encounter substantial challenges in spectrum sharing primarily due to their structural design and operational environments~\cite{jaroenjittichai2017radio .}
elements.sent-2411-349: Due to the physical limitations of using a solid, large single reflector antenna system, they lack the ability to process or differentiate these signals independently~\cite{leshem2000multichannel .}

elements.sent-2411-352: Each device is configured to simultaneously capture astronomical and communication signals at the same frequency, time, and location.

elements.sent-2411-356: Significance: The capability for efficient spectrum sharing between astronomical and communication signals unlocks new avenues for radio astronomy.

elements.sent-2411-362: These outcomes highlight the practicality and substantial benefits of the IoT-enabled design, paving the way for cost-effective, rapid, and widely accessible astronomical observations.

elements.subsubsection-2411-011: Node Path Differences for Finite Distance Calibrators
elements.sent-2411-377: Traditional interferometer radio telescopes are calibrated using well‐characterized astronomical sources, such as quasars, which are effectively at infinite distance.
elements.sent-2411-378: As described in Fig.~\ref{fig:distance_calibrator (a), the calibrator’s wavefront is nearly a perfect plane wave across the telescope’s aperture.}
elements.sent-2411-379: However, when a satellite is used as a calibration source in Fig.~\ref{fig:distance_calibrator (b), its finite distance results in a curved wavefront, which introduces phase discrepancies among the nodes relative to the ideal plane-wave condition.}
elements.figure-2411-024: \includegraphics[width=0.40\textwidth]{Figures/distance_calibrator.png} \caption{\textcolor{black}{Infinite distance calibrator vs. finite distance calibrator.}} \label{fig:distance_calibrator}
elements.sent-2411-380: By assigning each calibration satellite to a smaller subregion, the curvature of the satellite’s wavefront within that region approximates a uniform plane wave.
elements.sent-2411-381: Furthermore, GPS localization is used to accurately determine the positions of both the nodes and the satellites, thereby enabling precise phase corrections during calibration.
elements.sent-2411-382: The details of this method and the calculation results are described in Section~\ref{section:multiple_sat_calibration .. \textcolor{black {.}}}
elements.sent-2411-383: For a traditional interferometry array, the position of each array node must be known to within a fraction of the wavelength to ensure coherent signal combination.
elements.sent-2411-384: Even minor positional errors can cause significant phase misalignments, resulting in signal cancellation and reduced sensitivity.
elements.sent-2411-385: In contrast, the IoT-enabled telescope utilizes an eigenvalue-based calibration method that generates an effective beam in the calibrator's direction without requiring precise knowledge of each node's position.
elements.sent-2411-386: The details of the method and the calculation results are described in Section~\ref{section:system_signal_model .. \textcolor{black {.}}}
elements.subsubsection-2411-013: Extremely Precise Timing Requirements for Multi-Beam Arrays
elements.sent-2411-387: Traditional interferometry arrays employ hydrogen maser atomic clocks at each station to achieve synchronization at the femtosecond level~\cite{bandi2024comprehensive .}
elements.sent-2411-388: In contrast, IoT nodes rely on GPS timing, which can provides \textless1 nanosecond accuracy using GPS-disciplined oscillators (GPSDO)~\cite{ettusUB210 .}
elements.sent-2411-389: To compensate for the lower timing precision, IoT nodes perform frequent per-observation frequency calibration.
elements.sent-2411-390: As described in Section~\ref{section:system_signal_model , provided that the calibration is performed frequently enough, the frequency drift of the node clocks can be effectively mitigated before it significantly degrades coherence of the IoT radio telescope.. \textcolor{black {.}}}
elements.subsubsection-2411-014: RFI from Satellite Calibration Signals to Astronomical Signals
elements.sent-2411-391: The calibration signals transmitted by satellites could inadvertently overwhelm the weak cosmic signals that the array nodes are designed to detect.
elements.sent-2411-392: As illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:calibration_description , a time-division scheduling scheme isolates this interference.}
elements.sent-2411-393: The simulation model described in Section~\ref{section:system_signal_model implements this approach by having satellites transmit known calibration signals during brief calibration intervals.}
elements.sent-2411-394: During these intervals, nodes measure and update their phase offsets while refraining from collecting astronomical data.
elements.sent-2411-395: In the subsequent observation period, the satellites cease transmission, allowing nodes to record only natural cosmic signals with the calibration already applied.
elements.sent-2411-396: By rapidly toggling between calibration and observation, the system ensures that the strong satellite calibration signals do not drown out the astronomical signals.. \textcolor{black {.}}
elements.subsubsection-2411-015: RFI from Terrestrial Communication Signals to Satellite Calibration Signals
elements.sent-2411-397: Satellite calibration signals are encoded with distinct coded sequences that serve as identifiable signatures.
elements.sent-2411-398: IoT receivers leverage these known codes to accurately detect and isolate the calibration signals from any overlapping terrestrial communication signals.. \textcolor{black {.}}
elements.subsubsection-2411-016: RFI from Satellite Communication Signals to Astronomical Signals
elements.sent-2411-399: For predictable external RFI stemming from ground-to-satellite communications, dynamic beamforming with null-steering is employed.
elements.sent-2411-400: The array’s beamforming algorithms adaptively place nulls in the direction of known interfering satellites, effectively suppressing their signals.. \textcolor{black {.}}
elements.subsubsection-2411-017: RFI from Terrestrial Communication Signals to Astronomical Signals
elements.sent-2411-401: An amplitude clipping threshold is established just above the ambient noise floor.
elements.sent-2411-402: Any terrestrial communication signal that exceeds this threshold is automatically clipped.
elements.sent-2411-403: Since most astronomical signals are below the noise floor of the receiving system, this approach mitigates the impact of strong terrestrial signals, enabling the system to focus on sub-noise-floor signals.
elements.sent-2411-404: This strategy permits the telescope array to operate concurrently with other services within shared frequency bands.
elements.sent-2411-405: Consequently, IoT devices can continue performing their primary communication functions (e.g., Wi-Fi, cellular) while simultaneously contributing to scientific observations.
elements.sent-2411-406: The details of this method and the calculation results are presented in Section V.. \textcolor{black {.}}
elements.subsection-2411-030: Grating Lobes for a Large-Scale Array
elements.sent-2411-407: Grating lobes typically appear in uniform arrays when the element spacing exceeds half the wavelength.
elements.sent-2411-408: However, for a large-scale random array, the sidelobe region of the radiation pattern consists of a sum of random phasors due to the irregular distribution of array elements.
elements.sent-2411-409: This randomness leads to a significant reduction in the peak sidelobe level compared to that of a uniform array.
elements.sent-2411-410: As noted in classical work by Y.. T.
elements.sent-2411-411: Lo~\cite{lo1964mathematical , consider a square array operating at 300 GHz with an aperture equivalent to the Earth's diameter and a desired peak sidelobe level (PSLL) of -30 dB.}
elements.sent-2411-412: For a random array, approximately $5\times10^{5}$ elements are sufficient, whereas a uniform array with half-wavelength spacing would require about $2\times10^{20}$ elements.
elements.sent-2411-413: Moreover, according to~\cite{diao2017sidelobe , the PSLL can be approximately calculated by \begin{equation}\text{PSLL (dB)} = -10 \log_{10} \left( D_{\text{ele}} \frac{N_{\text{ele}}}{L/\lambda} \right),\end{equation} where $D_{\text{ele }$ represents the element directivity in the mainlobe direction, $N_{\text{ele }$ is the number of array elements, and $L$ is the array length.}}}
elements.sent-2411-414: Assuming $D_{\text{ele =1$, $N_{\text{ele =5\times10^{10}$, $L=1\times10^{7}$ meters, and an operating frequency of 1.42 GHz, the PSLL is approximately -30 dB.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-415: Therefore, this rough estimation indicates that grating lobes are unlikely to occur, and the sidelobe levels in the large-scale IoT-enabled telescope can be maintained at very low levels due to the random distribution of nodes.. \textcolor{black {.}}
elements.subsection-2411-031: High Data Throughput from Many Nodes
elements.sent-2411-416: Aggregating data from a vast number of IoT nodes generates an enormous data volume.
elements.sent-2411-417: Each node in the radio telescope array produces high-bandwidth signal streams, and the cumulative data can exceed the throughput and storage capabilities of conventional networks and data centers.
elements.sent-2411-418: To maintain scalability, instead of funneling all raw data centrally, each IoT node performs pre-processing before transmission.
elements.sent-2411-419: For example, a node can send time-averaged raw astronomical data to the data center.
elements.sent-2411-420: Additionally, for non-real-time observation, data transfers can be scheduled during off-peak internet usage hours and periods of lower electricity costs, thereby further reducing operational expenses.. \textcolor{black {.}}
elements.sent-2411-421: To enhance the efficiency of correlation and beamforming calculations in a large-scale array, a multi-layer digital beamforming strategy is employed, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:system_overview (a) right.}
elements.sent-2411-422: In this scheme, the global IoT network is divided into several smaller regions, or subarrays, where initial beamforming weights are computed over the half spherical scanning range for each local subarray.
elements.sent-2411-423: The locally beamformed signals are subsequently combined and processed through an additional layer of digital beamforming.
elements.sent-2411-424: This hierarchical approach optimizes the distribution of computations, reduces the processing load on individual nodes, and minimizes the required data transmission bandwidth, thereby enhancing overall processing efficiency and scalability.. \textcolor{black {.}}
elements.subsection-2411-033: Potential Risk of Location and User Data Leakage
elements.sent-2411-425: Integrating personal IoT devices into a scientific sensing network raises significant data privacy and security concerns, as these devices may capture or be linked to personally identifiable information.
elements.sent-2411-426: To address these issues, since most astronomical signals are below the noise floor of the IoT node receiver system, only anonymized noise-level astronomical data is sent to the central processor, protecting both user confidentiality and the integrity of the scientific data.. \textcolor{black {.}}
elements.sent-2411-427: To maximize the operational efficiency of the distributed IoT-based radio telescope network and foster public engagement, an incentive plan should be implemented to encourage individuals to contribute their personal devices—such as smartphones and laptops—as active nodes in the array.
elements.sent-2411-428: This approach transforms everyday technology into valuable resources for astronomical research.
elements.sent-2411-429: Participants would receive compensation through digital credits, service discounts, or monetary payments determined by their contributed processing power and the duration of device participation.
elements.sent-2411-430: Furthermore, it is advisable to establish a protocol or standard to ensure that future IoT devices are designed to support the frequency and bandwidth requirements essential for radio astronomy, such as the 1.4\,GHz band.
elements.sent-2411-431: The additional costs associated with this enhancement could be offset by auctioning the 1.4\,GHz bandwidth, which is currently reserved for radio astronomy, or by reallocating funds from the construction of new radio telescopes—given that the IoT-enabled telescope leverages nearly free infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-432: By allowing participants to determine their level of involvement based on personal preference, this strategy broadens the community of citizen scientists.
elements.sent-2411-433: Overall, the incorporation of such an incentive plan not only enhances network scalability but also fosters public collaboration in scientific discovery.
elements.sent-2411-434: This democratization of astronomical research is expected to boost public interest in science and technology, provide valuable educational opportunities, and promote STEM engagement across diverse populations.

elements.sent-2411-444: In this section, we present a comprehensive signal model to better understand the proposed IoT-enabled telescope.
elements.sent-2411-445: The model captures the entire signal chain of each IoT node, from reception to final beamformed output, while incorporating hardware and system noise variability, local oscillator (LO) offset, interference mitigation via ADC thresholds, time-division calibration/observation scheduling, calibration methods, and output SNR calculations.
elements.sent-2411-446: We consider a one-dimensional array of $N$ IoT nodes randomly distributed along the $x$-axis over an aperture length $L$.
elements.sent-2411-447: The position of the $n$-th node is given by \begin{equation}x_n = L \, (u_n - 0.5),\end{equation} where $u_n \sim \mathcal{U [0,1]$ is a uniform random variable.}
elements.sent-2411-448: Each node is equipped with an antenna and a receiver front-end whose performance is characterized by variability in antenna gain and noise figure.
elements.sent-2411-449: In particular, we model the antenna gain (in dB) as \begin{equation}\label{eq:Gn} G_{n,\mathrm{dB}} = G_{\mathrm{ant}} + \sigma_G \, z^G_n,\end{equation} and the noise figure (in dB) as \begin{equation}\label{eq:NF_n} \mathrm{NF}_{n,\mathrm{dB}} = \mathrm{NF}_0 + \sigma_{\mathrm{NF}} \, z^{\mathrm{NF}}_n,\end{equation} where $G_{\mathrm{ant }$ and $\mathrm{NF _0$ are the nominal antenna gain and noise figure, $\sigma_G$ and $\sigma_{\mathrm{NF }$ are their standard deviations, and $z^G_n$, $z^{\mathrm{NF _n \sim \mathcal{N (0,1)$ are independent standard normal random variables.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-451: The effective antenna area at node $n$ is computed as \begin{equation}A_{\mathrm{eff},n} = \frac{G_n \, \lambda_c^2}{4\pi},\end{equation} where $\lambda_c = c/f_c$ is the wavelength at the center frequency $f_c$.
elements.sent-2411-452: Each node's receiver noise temperature is modeled by \begin{equation}T_{\mathrm{noise},n} = T_0 \cdot \mathrm{NF}_n,\end{equation} with $T_0=290$~K.
elements.sent-2411-453: The corresponding noise power over a bandwidth $B$ is \begin{equation}P_{\mathrm{noise},n} = k_B \, T_{\mathrm{noise},n} \, B,\end{equation} where $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant.
elements.sent-2411-454: During the calibration-on period, the calibration signal received at node $n$ is modeled as \begin{equation}\label{eq:s_cal} s^{\mathrm{cal-on}}_n(t) = A^{\mathrm{cal}}_n \cos\Big(2\pi f_c t - k_c\,x_n\cos\theta_{\mathrm{cal}}\Big),\end{equation} where \begin{equation}A^{\mathrm{cal}}_n = \sqrt{2\,P_{\mathrm{cal}}\,A_{\mathrm{eff},n}},\end{equation} $P_{\mathrm{cal }$ is the calibration signal power density, $k_c = \frac{2\pi {\lambda_c}$, and $\theta_{\mathrm{cal }$ is the known direction of the calibration signal.}}}
elements.sent-2411-455: During the observation period, the astronomical signal is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:s_obs} s^{\mathrm{obs}}_n(t) = A^{\mathrm{obs}}_n \cos\Big(2\pi f_c t - k_c\,x_n\cos\theta_{s}\Big),\end{equation} with \begin{equation}A^{\mathrm{obs}}_n = \sqrt{2\,P_{\mathrm{astro}}\,A_{\mathrm{eff},n}},\end{equation} where $P_{\mathrm{astro }$ is the astronomical signal power density and $\theta_{s}$ is its direction of arrival.}
elements.sent-2411-456: The received signal at node $n$ is subject to additive noise $n_n(t)$, modeled as a zero-mean Gaussian process with variance $\sigma_n^2 = P_{\mathrm{noise ,n}$: \begin{equation}n_n(t) \sim \mathcal{N}\big(0,\; \sigma_n^2\big).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-457: Furthermore, interference from external sources is incorporated into the model.
elements.sent-2411-458: Assuming there are $Q$ interferers, the interference at node $n$ is modeled as \begin{equation}i_n(t) = \sum_{q=1}^{Q} A^{\mathrm{int}}_{q,n} \cos\Big(2\pi f_{q} t - k_c\,x_n\cos\theta_{q} + \varphi_q\Big),\end{equation} where the amplitude of the $q$-th interferer at node $n$ is given by \begin{equation}A^{\mathrm{int}}_{q,n} = \sqrt{2\,P^{\mathrm{int}}_{q}\,A_{\mathrm{eff},n}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-459: Here, $P^{\mathrm{int _{q}$ denotes the interference power density of the $q$-th interferer, $f_{q}$ is its carrier frequency, $\theta_{q}$ is its direction of arrival, and $\varphi_q$ is an arbitrary phase.}}
elements.sent-2411-460: Thus, the total RF signal received at node $n$ is \begin{equation}\label{eq:r_n_rf} r_n(t) = s_n(t) + i_n(t) + n_n(t),\end{equation} where \begin{equation}s_n(t) =  s^{cal-on}_n(t), during calibration-on, [1mm] s^{obs}_n(t), during observation. \end{equation} In other words, each node receives its desired signal $s_n(t)$ (either the calibration signal or the astronomical signal) superimposed with the aggregate interference from all $Q$ external sources and additive noise.. \textcolor{black {.}}

elements.sent-2411-461: To mitigate the impact of strong interference, an amplitude clipping operation is applied prior to digitization.
elements.sent-2411-462: Specifically, if the magnitude of $r_n(t)$ exceeds a threshold $V_{\mathrm{th }$, it is clipped: \begin{equation}\label{eq:clipping} r_{n,\mathrm{clipped}}(t) = \begin{cases} V_{\mathrm{th}}, r_n(t) > V_{\mathrm{th}}, [1mm] -V_{\mathrm{th}}, r_n(t) < -V_{\mathrm{th}}, [1mm] r_n(t), \text{otherwise}. \end{cases}\end{equation} This prevents strong interferers from saturating the ADC and increasing system noise after the beamforming while preserving the weak astronomical signal.. \textcolor{black {.}}}
elements.formula-2411-077: \begin{equation}\label{eq:clipping} r_{n,clipped}(t) =  V_{th}, r_n(t) > V_{th}, [1mm] -V_{th}, r_n(t) < -V_{th}, [1mm] r_n(t), otherwise. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-463: Each node downconverts the RF signal to baseband using its LO.
elements.sent-2411-464: Although ideally $f_{LO =f_c$, each node experiences a small frequency offset $\Delta f_n$ and an initial phase offset $\phi_n$, so that \begin{equation}f_{LO,n} = f_c + \Delta f_n.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-465: The mixing operation is performed by multiplying the clipped signal by the LO complex exponential: \begin{equation}m_n(t) = e^{-j\,(2\pi f_{LO,n} t + \phi_n)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-466: After mixing and ideal low-pass filtering, the complex baseband signal is obtained.
elements.sent-2411-467: For the calibration signal, this yields \begin{equation}\label{eq:y_cal_base} y^{\mathrm{cal-on}}_n(t) = \frac{A^{\mathrm{cal}}_n}{2}\, e^{-j\,(k_c\,x_n\cos\theta_{\mathrm{cal}} + \phi_n + 2\pi \Delta f_n t)} + \tilde{n}^{\mathrm{cal}}_n(t),\end{equation} where $\tilde{n ^{\mathrm{cal _n(t)$ represents the filtered noise.}}}
elements.sent-2411-468: Here, we assume that the calibration signal can be distinguished from interference by utilizing its known code sequence.
elements.sent-2411-469: Similarly, for the astronomical signal during observation, we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:y_obs_base} y^{\mathrm{obs}}_n(t) = \frac{A^{\mathrm{obs}}_n}{2}\, e^{-j\,(k_c\,x_n\cos\theta_{s} + \phi_n + 2\pi \Delta f_n t)} + \tilde{n}^{\mathrm{obs}}_n(t) + \tilde{i}_n(t),\end{equation} with $\tilde{n ^{\mathrm{obs _n(t)$ and $\tilde{i _n(t)$ being the baseband noise and interference components, respectively.}}}}

elements.subsection-2411-039: Time-Division for Calibration and Observation Modes
elements.sent-2411-471: The system alternates between two modes:.
elements.sent-2411-472: Calibration Mode: Over a calibration cycle of duration $T_{\mathrm{cal }$, a transmitter emits a known signal for a period $T_{\mathrm{cal- on $, with the remaining time \begin{equation}T_{cal-off} = T_{cal} - T_{cal-on}\end{equation} used to measure noise.}}}
elements.sent-2411-473: During the calibration-on period, the received signals at each node are employed to estimate phase and amplitude differences across the array.
elements.sent-2411-474: Observation Mode: After calibration, an observation period of duration $T_{\mathrm{obs }$ is used to acquire astronomical data.}
elements.sent-2411-475: During this period, each node receives the weak astronomical signal in addition to noise and interference.
elements.sent-2411-476: ADC clipping limits strong interference, and the downconverted baseband signals are combined using the calibration-derived beamforming weights.
elements.sent-2411-477: This time-division scheme prevents strong calibration signals from contaminating astronomical observations while enabling accurate noise measurement and calibration.
elements.sent-2411-478: To enable coherent combination across the array, calibration is performed using the calibration signal.
elements.sent-2411-480: In PAC, the relative phase differences between nodes are estimated via cross-correlation during the calibration-on period.. Let \begin{equation}y_n^{\mathrm{cal-on}}(t) \approx \frac{A^{\mathrm{cal}}_n}{2}\, e^{-j\,[k_c\,x_n\cos\theta_{\mathrm{cal}} + \phi_n]} + \tilde{n}_n^{\mathrm{cal-on}}(t).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-481: The phase difference between node $n$ and a reference node (say, node 1) is estimated as \begin{equation}\hat{\Delta\phi}_n = \arg\!\left\{ \frac{1}{T_{\mathrm{cal-on}}} \int_{0}^{T_{\mathrm{cal-on}}} y_n^{\mathrm{cal-on}}(t) \Bigl( y_1^{\mathrm{cal-on}}(t) \Bigr)^* dt \right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-482: Beamforming weights are then set as \begin{equation}w_n = e^{-j \hat{\Delta\phi}_n}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-483: During observation, the combined array output is \begin{equation}y_{\mathrm{combined}}^{\mathrm{PAC}}(t) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \, y_n^{\mathrm{obs}}(t).\end{equation}.
