elements.sent-2411-486: Similarly, the signal-plus-noise covariance during calibration-on is \begin{equation}\mathbf{R}_{\mathrm{sn}} = \frac{1}{T_{\mathrm{cal-on}}} \int_{0}^{T_{\mathrm{cal-on}}} \mathbf{y}^{\mathrm{cal-on}}(t) \, \mathbf{y}^{\mathrm{cal-on}}(t)^\dagger dt.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-488: The optimal weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ is obtained by solving \begin{equation}\mathbf{R}_s \, \mathbf{w} = \lambda_{\max} \left( \mathbf{R}_n + \mathbf{I} \right) \mathbf{w},\end{equation} where $\mathbf{I}$ is the identity matrix and $\lambda_{\max}$ is the maximum eigenvalue.
elements.sent-2411-489: The corresponding eigenvector is normalized as \begin{equation}\mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{norm}} = \frac{\mathbf{w}}{\left( \sum_{n=1}^{N} |w_n| \right)/N}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-491: During the observation period, after applying the calibration-derived beamforming weights to the downconverted signals, the combined output at the array is given by \begin{equation}y_{\mathrm{combined}}^{\mathrm{obs}}(t) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n\, y_n^{\mathrm{obs}}(t),\end{equation} where $w_n$ are the beamforming weights (obtained via PAC or EVC) and $y_n^{\mathrm{obs (t)$ is the complex baseband astronomical signal (including noise and any residual interference) at node $n$, as in Eq.~\eqref{eq:y_obs_base .}}}
elements.sent-2411-492: To quantify the performance during observation, we define the signal power $P_s$ as the squared magnitude of the time-integrated coherent astronomical signal component.
elements.sent-2411-493: In practice, since the astronomical signal is much weaker than the noise, the signal power is obtained by isolating the known signal component (e.g., via a signal-only processing path): \begin{equation}P_s = \left|\int_{0}^{T_{\mathrm{obs}}} y_{\mathrm{combined}}^{\mathrm{obs,sig}}(t)\, dt \right|^2,\end{equation} where $y_{\mathrm{combined ^{\mathrm{obs,sig (t)$ denotes the beamformed signal when only the astronomical component is present.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-494: Similarly, the total received signal power during the observation period is defined as \begin{equation}P_{\rm{obs}} = \left|\int_{0}^{T_{\mathrm{obs}}} y_{\mathrm{combined}}^{\mathrm{obs}}(t)\, dt \right|^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-495: The noise power, $P_n$, is then obtained by subtracting the signal power $P_s$ from the observed power: \begin{equation}P_n = P_{\rm{obs}} - P_s.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-513: This section presents a comparative analysis of the proposed IoT-based radio telescope and the FAST, focusing on key performance metrics such as antenna gain, system noise temperature, sensitivity, field of view (FoV), bandwidth, beamwidth, and survey speed.
elements.sent-2411-514: The aim is to evaluate the potential of the IoT-enabled telescope concept relative to a state-of-the-art radio telescope.
elements.sent-2411-515: The performance parameters considered for comparison include the antenna gain $G$, which represents the gain in the sky direction and incorporates the antenna radiation efficiency $\eta_{\text{rad }$ and blockage effects $L_{\text{path }$ due to environmental obstacles like buildings and trees.}}

elements.sent-2411-517: Sensitivity $S$ is defined as \begin{equation}S = A / T_{\text{sys}},\end{equation} where $A$ is the antenna effective area.
elements.sent-2411-518: The FoV is the angular area over which the telescope can detect signals, approximated by \begin{equation}\text{FoV} = N_{\text{effb}} (\lambda / D)^2,\end{equation} where $N_{\text{effb }$ is the number of effective beams, $\lambda$ is the wavelength, and $D$ is the diameter of the telescope.}
elements.sent-2411-519: Beamwidth $\theta_{\text{bw }$ is given by $\theta_{\text{bw \approx \lambda / D$, representing the angular width of the main lobe of the antenna pattern.}}}
elements.sent-2411-520: Bandwidth $B$ is the frequency range over which the telescope operates.
elements.sent-2411-521: Survey speed (SS) is calculated as \begin{equation}\text{SS} = S^2 \text{FoV},\end{equation} reflecting the speed at which the telescope can survey the sky.. \textcolor{black {.}}
elements.sent-2411-522: The FAST telescope is the world's largest single-dish telescope, featuring a diameter of 500 meters.

elements.sent-2411-534: Despite each individual IoT device having a low antenna gain due to the large antenna loss (-6 dB) and building blockage effect (-10 dB), the cumulative effect of aggregating signals from an immense number of devices results in a total antenna gain that is almost two orders of magnitude higher than that of the FAST.
elements.sent-2411-535: The system noise temperature of the IoT-enabled telescope is higher because IoT devices typically have greater noise figures, and the external environmental noise temperature is approximately equal to room temperature ($300\,\text{K}$).
elements.sent-2411-536: However, the IoT-enabled telescope offers a vastly larger field of view, effectively covering almost the entire sky simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-537: In contrast, the FAST has a much smaller FoV due to its highly directional antenna.
elements.sent-2411-538: Consequently, the IoT-enabled telescope achieves a survey speed that is nearly seven orders of magnitude greater than that of the FAST, attributed to the large number of nodes and the expansive FoV.
elements.sent-2411-539: In practical terms, a survey that might take FAST approximate 10 million hours, or 1142 years, could be done in 1 hour with the proposed IoT-enabled telescope.

elements.sent-2411-540: Notably, compared with the DSA‑2000, which currently exhibits the highest survey speed among systems under construction, the proposed IoT-enabled telescope is projected to achieve a survey speed that is five orders of magnitude higher.
elements.sent-2411-541: In contrast, IoT-enabled radio telescopes leverage existing IoT infrastructure as receivers and the potential use of existing low-orbit satellites as calibrators, thereby eliminating the need for expensive, dedicated systems.
elements.sent-2411-542: Consequently, these systems can operate in interference-rich environments, enabling efficient spectrum sharing with wireless communication networks.
elements.sent-2411-543: This paper introduces an innovative approach to radio astronomy by leveraging the global IoT network to build a distributed radio telescope.
elements.sent-2411-544: Utilizing a vast array of dispersed nodes, the system overcomes the spectrum sharing and coverage constraints of traditional single-aperture telescopes.
elements.sent-2411-545: Digital beamforming, coupled with GPS-synchronized calibration via satellite beacons, effectively aligns astronomical signals and mitigates phase errors.
elements.sent-2411-546: Performance assessments indicate that Eigenvalue-Based Calibration (EVC) outperforms Phase Alignment Calibration (PAC) under variable node conditions, while setting the ADC threshold near the noise floor minimizes interference.
elements.sent-2411-547: Furthermore, the adverse impact of local oscillator (LO) frequency offsets is reduced by shortening observation durations.
elements.sent-2411-548: In comparison to the 500-meter Aperture Spherical Telescope (FAST), the IoT-enabled design achieves over two orders of magnitude higher antenna gain and nearly seven orders of magnitude faster survey speed, thanks to its extensive effective aperture and all-sky field-of-view.
elements.sent-2411-549: These findings validate a cost-effective, scalable platform for radio astronomy, with future work focusing on prototyping with software-defined radios and addressing challenges in synchronization, data processing, and interference management.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
